ACCESS CONTROL & INIT — - **ERC4337FactoryWrapper::deploy** — Restricts deployment to `senderCreator` only — If `senderCreator` is compromised or set incorrectly, all wallet deployments are blocked or hijacked - **Estimator::_isValidImage** — Always returns `true` overriding validation — Any signature/imageHash accepted; attacker can execute arbitrary payloads - **Hooks::addHook** — Hook can overwrite existing selector if check fails — Race condition or check bypass allows hook replacement, enabling malicious delegatecall target - **Hooks::removeHook** — No validation that caller owns hook — Could be bypassed if `_readHook` returns stale data - **Implementation::updateImplementation** — No validation of `_implementation` address — Can set to zero or malicious address, bricking or hijacking wallet - **Recovery::queuePayload** — No rate limiting or array-length caps on `queuedPayloadHashes` — Griefing via unbounded array growth (DoS) - **SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature** — External caller is `msg.sender` (wallet) but no authentication beyond that — If wallet proxy is misconfigured, unauthorized calls possible

ARITHMETIC — - **SessionSig::recoverConfiguration** — `permissionsCount` incremented unchecked, could exceed array length if `maxPermissionsSize` guess is wrong — Out-of-bounds write risk, memory corruption - **SessionSig::_decodePermissions** — Reads `uint8 length`, but no check that encoded data has enough bytes — Underflow/out-of-bounds read if malformed - **LibBytes (implicit via SessionSig)** — Multiple `readUintX` / `readBytes32` assume sufficient data, but no boundary checks in some paths — Truncation or revert on short input - **PermissionValidator::validatePermission** — `uint256 value256 = uint256(value)` cast from `bytes32` — Potential data loss if `mask` is incorrect - **SessionSig::recoverSignature** — Pointer arithmetic `pointer += ...` unchecked in several places — Could wrap or exceed `encodedSignature.length` causing OOB - **Payload::fromPackedCalls** — `nonceSize` derived from bitfield `(globalFlag >> 1) & 0x07` — If `nonceSize` is 0, nonce is zero; no validation that this is intended - **LibBytes::readUintX** — `_sizeBytes` controls how many bytes to read, but if `_sizeBytes > 32`, no revert — Could read garbage or revert unpredictably - **SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature** — `sessionUsageLimits` array resized via assembly `mstore(actualSessionUsageLimits, actualSize)` — Off-by-one or incorrect size could cause memory overlap - **ExplicitSessionManager::_validateLimitUsageIncrement** — `uint256 totalLimitsLength` summed without overflow check — Very large arrays could wrap, leading to incorrect array allocation - **Payload::fromPackedCalls** — `numCalls` read as uint8 or uint16, but no check against maximum reasonable limit — Could allocate huge `Call[]` array, DoS - **Nonce::_consumeNonce** — `unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1 }` — Nonce wraps at `2^256-1`, allowing replay after wrap - **SessionSig::recoverConfiguration** — `blacklistCount` read from nibble or uint16, but no cap — Could allocate huge `address[]`, DoS

TOKEN ACCOUNTING — - **Calls::_execute** — No checks on return values of external calls (via `LibOptim.call`) — Fee-on-transfer or revert-on-zero-approval tokens could fail silently - **ERC4337v07::validateUserOp** — `missingAccountFunds` sent via `depositTo` without checking wallet balance — Could revert if wallet has insufficient balance, blocking UserOp - **Guest::_dispatchGuest** — Native token sent via `call.value` without checking balance — Could fail silently or revert, leaving inconsistent state - **Hooks::receive** — Accepts ETH without event or tracking — Griefing or lost funds if no withdraw mechanism - **Calls::_execute** — `call.value` transferred without verifying `address(this).balance >= call.value` — Could revert if balance insufficient - **SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature** — `sessionUsageLimits[i].totalValueUsed` incremented without checking wallet balance — Limit enforcement off-chain only, on-chain state could diverge

ARRAY / INDEX / INPUT VALIDATION — - **SessionSig::recoverSignature** — `attestationIndex` read from `uint8`, checked `< attestationList.length`, but `attestationList` built from `attestationCount` which is unbounded — Large `attestationCount` could cause OOB - **SessionSig::recoverConfiguration** — `blacklistCount` read from nibble (0-14) or uint16, but no validation against reasonable max — DoS via huge array - **SessionSig::_decodePermissions** — `length` read as uint8, but no validation that encoded data has `length * MIN_ENCODED_PERMISSION_SIZE` bytes — OOB read - **Simulator::simulate** — `_calls` array length not validated — Could run out of gas or exceed block limit - **Estimator::estimate** — `decoded.calls` length not validated — DoS via large payload - **Recovery::queuePayload** — `queuedPayloadHashes[_wallet][_signer]` unbounded array — DoS via unlimited queuing - **ImplicitSessionManager::_isAddressBlacklisted** — Binary search assumes sorted array, but only one check in `recoverConfiguration` — If blacklist is unsorted, blacklist check fails - **PermissionValidator::validatePermission** — `permission.rules.length` unbounded — DoS via large rule array - **Payload::fromPackedCalls** — `numCalls` up to uint16 (65535) — Allocating 65k `Call[]` could exhaust memory or gas - **SessionSig::recoverConfiguration** — `maxPermissionsSize = encoded.length / MIN_ENCODED_PERMISSION_SIZE` — Could allocate massive array if `encoded.length` is large, DoS - **SessionSig::recoverSignature** — `callsCount = payload.calls.length` used to size `sig.callSignatures` — No check that `callsCount` matches encoded signature length, could mismatch - **LibPermission::readPermission** — `rulesLength` read as uint8, but no check that `encoded[pointer:]` has enough data — OOB read

EXTERNAL CALLS & REENTRANCY — - **Calls::_execute** — Calls external contracts via `LibOptim.call` and `delegatecall` without reentrancy guard on entire function — CEI violated if `delegateCall` target calls back - **ReentrancyGuard** — Uses storage slot to prevent reentrancy, but `nonReentrant` only applied to `execute`, not `executeUserOp` or `selfExecute` — Reentrancy possible via ERC4337 or self-calls - **Guest::_dispatchGuest** — No reentrancy guard — External calls can re-enter wallet - **Simulator::simulate** — No reentrancy guard — Malicious target can re-enter and manipulate state - **Estimator::estimate** — No reentrancy guard — Reentrancy during gas estimation could skew results - **Hooks::fallback** — Delegatecalls to hook without reentrancy protection — Hook can call back into wallet - **Calls::_execute** — `delegateCall` passes control to untrusted extension — Extension can call any function in wallet, bypassing access controls - **Recovery::queuePayload** — Calls `isValidSignature` on `_signer` if code.length > 0 — Reentrancy if signer is malicious contract - **ERC4337v07::validateUserOp** — Calls `isValidSignature` which may call external contracts — Reentrancy during validation - **ERC4337v07::executeUserOp** — Calls `selfExecute` which executes payload — Reentrancy via payload calls - **LibOptim.call / delegatecall (inferred)** — No inline checks on success/revert data — Malicious target can return fake success or revert with manipulated data

CROSS-CHAIN / BRIDGE — - **Payload::domainSeparator** — `_noChainId` flag allows omitting chainId — Signature replayable across chains if noChainId=true - **SessionPermissions::chainId** — If `chainId == 0`, session valid on any chain — Cross-chain replay possible - **ERC4337v07::validateUserOp** — No chainId validation in userOp — Replay on different chains if EntryPoint deployed at same address - **SessionSig::recoverSignature** — `wallet` used in payload hash, but no chainId enforcement — Cross-chain replay if wallet deployed at same address - **Recovery::recoveryPayloadHash** — Uses `_payload.noChainId` — If true, signature valid on all chains

ECONOMIC / INCENTIVE LOGIC — - **SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature** — Session usage limits tracked per-wallet but not enforced atomically across multiple calls — Attacker can exceed limits by reordering or splitting transactions - **ExplicitSessionManager::incrementUsageLimit** — Caller can increment usage without validation that they consumed it — Griefing by pre-incrementing limits - **ExplicitSessionManager::_validateLimitUsageIncrement** — Expected increment call at index 0, but no validation that it's actually executed first — Attacker can skip increment by setting `onlyFallback` or `behaviorOnError` - **Recovery::queuePayload** — `requiredDeltaTime` can be 0, allowing instant recovery — Attacker can bypass intended delay - **Recovery::_recoverBranch** — `queuedAt` compared to `minTimestamp`, but no check that `minTimestamp` is reasonable — Attacker can set `minTimestamp=0` and bypass queue - **PermissionValidator::validatePermission** — Cumulative limits stored per `keccak256(signer, permission, i)`, but no salt — Collision if same signer+permission used in different sessions - **Nonce::readNonce** — Nonce can be read without incrementing, allowing off-chain simulation — But nonce space can be griefed by front-running with different space - **Calls::_execute** — `call.gasLimit` can be 0, passing all remaining gas — Gas griefing by forcing all gas to be consumed - **Payload::fromPackedCalls** — No minimum nonce increment enforced — Attacker can reuse nonce=0 if never incremented (edge case) - **SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature** — `MAX_SPACE = type(uint80).max - 1` but no enforcement that `payload.space <= MAX_SPACE` in payload decoding — Invalid space accepted - **ERC4337v07::validateUserOp** — `missingAccountFunds` deposited to EntryPoint, but no tracking of deposited amount — Attacker can drain wallet by repeatedly calling with high `missingAccountFunds` - **Passkeys::recoverSapientSignatureCompact** — No check on `_requireUserVerification` flag consistency — Attacker can bypass user verification by flipping flag - **WebAuthn (inferred)** — Challenge/response not validated against expected challenge — Replay or cross-origin attacks if challenge reused --- **Note:** Many issues stem from the complex signature recovery logic, lack of input validation on array lengths, and missing reentrancy guards on critical paths. The session management system has multiple economic attack vectors via limit manipulation. Cross-chain replay is possible if `noChainId=true` or `chainId=0`. The reentrancy guard is only applied to `execute`, leaving `executeUserOp`, `selfExecute`, and hook delegatecalls unprotected. ===== CHUNK 2/2 ===== # Findings

ACCESS CONTROL & INIT — - [ACCESS CONTROL] BaseAuth::setStaticSignature — onlySelf modifier allows wallet to set arbitrary static signatures — malicious wallet owner can pre-approve future operations without additional signatures - [ACCESS CONTROL] BaseAuth::updateImageHash — onlySelf allows wallet to change imageHash arbitrarily — wallet can change auth config to malicious one bypassing current signers - [ACCESS CONTROL] Stage1Auth::_updateImageHash — imageHash update automatically switches to STAGE_2_IMPLEMENTATION — if STAGE_2_IMPLEMENTATION is malicious/buggy, wallet is bricked after first update - [ACCESS CONTROL] Stage2Auth::_updateImageHash — no validation that new imageHash corresponds to valid signers — wallet can be locked out if imageHash set to uncontrolled value

ARITHMETIC — - [ARITHMETIC] BaseSig::recoverBranch — unchecked addition `weight += addrWeight` inside unchecked block — could overflow if malicious signature provides many FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH/FLAG_SIGNATURE_ERC1271 parts - [ARITHMETIC] BaseSig::recoverBranch — unchecked addition `weight += nweight` for FLAG_BRANCH — recursive branch could accumulate weight past uint256 max - [ARITHMETIC] BaseSig::recoverBranch — unchecked addition `weight += externalWeight` for FLAG_NESTED — malicious nested configs can overflow total weight - [ARITHMETIC] BaseSig::recover — checkpoint size read as `(signatureFlag & 0x1c) >> 2` max 7 bytes — if readUintX with size 0 returns dirty data, checkpoint could be wrong - [ARITHMETIC] BaseSig::recover — threshold size `((signatureFlag & 0x20) >> 5) + 1` is 1 or 2 bytes — readUintX with 0 bytes would skip read, threshold could be dirty stack value - [ARITHMETIC] LibBytes::readUintX — `sub(shl(mul(8, _length), 1), 1)` mask — if _length is 0, mask is `sub(1,1) = 0`, returns 0 instead of dirty data - [ARITHMETIC] BaseAuth::_setStaticSignature — `(_timestamp & 0xffffffffffffffffffffffff)` truncates timestamp to 96 bits — timestamp beyond year ~2500 AD wraps around

ARRAY / INDEX / INPUT VALIDATION — - [INPUT VALIDATION] BaseSig::recover — no bounds check on `checkpointSize` before calling `readUintX(rindex, checkpointSize)` — OOB read if signature crafted with size > remaining bytes - [INPUT VALIDATION] BaseSig::recoverBranch — `rindex < _signature.length` loop condition — if signature malformed, rindex can advance past length, reading dirty data - [INPUT VALIDATION] LibBytes::readUintX — no check that `_index + _length <= _data.length` — reads dirty data beyond calldata if length exceeds remaining bytes - [INPUT VALIDATION] LibBytes::readAddress — no check `_index + 20 <= _data.length` — OOB read returns dirty memory - [INPUT VALIDATION] LibBytes::readBytes32 — no check `_index + 32 <= _data.length` — OOB read returns dirty memory - [INPUT VALIDATION] BaseSig::recoverBranch — FLAG_BRANCH reads size with `readUintX(rindex, sizeSize)` then recurses into `[rindex:nrindex]` — if sizeSize is 0, size is 0, recurses into empty slice, may return garbage root - [INPUT VALIDATION] BaseSig::recoverChained — reads sigSize with `readUint24(rindex)`, then processes `[rindex:nrindex]` — no check that nrindex <= _signature.length, OOB read - [INPUT VALIDATION] BaseSig::recoverBranch — FLAG_NESTED reads size with `readUint24(rindex)` then recurses — no check that nrindex <= _signature.length, OOB read

ECONOMIC / INCENTIVE LOGIC — - [INCENTIVE] BaseAuth::_getStaticSignature — static signature expires at `timestamp` but uses `block.timestamp` — miner can manipulate block.timestamp to extend or shorten validity window by ~15 seconds - [INCENTIVE] BaseAuth::signatureValidation — static signature with `addr == address(0)` allows any caller — intended as "no restriction", but means anyone can use that operation - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverBranch — FLAG_SUBDIGEST sets `weight = type(uint256).max` if `hardcoded == _opHash` — malicious signer could precompute opHash and add subdigest node to always pass threshold - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverBranch — FLAG_SIGNATURE_ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST compares against `_payload.hashFor(address(0))` — allows counter-factual payloads, but if wallet not deployed yet, signature valid for any address - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverChained — chained signatures must have decreasing checkpoints — but if attacker controls all signatures, can craft chain that passes validation but represents old config - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recover — snapshot unused if `snapshot.imageHash == imageHash` or `checkpoint > snapshot.checkpoint` — checkpointer can return stale snapshot to make old config appear valid - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverBranch — FLAG_NESTED with `internalWeight >= internalThreshold` adds `externalWeight` — attacker can craft nested tree with low internalThreshold to cheaply gain externalWeight - [INCENTIVE] Stage1Auth::_isValidImage — checks wallet address matches `CREATE2(FACTORY, _imageHash, INIT_CODE_HASH)` — if FACTORY compromised, can deploy wallet at predicted address with malicious imageHash - [INCENTIVE] BaseAuth::recoverSapientSignature — appends `msg.sender` to `parentWallets` array — if msg.sender is attacker contract, can inject arbitrary parent into validation chain - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverChained — `_snapshot.imageHash = bytes32(0)` after match — if snapshot reused across validations, second validation skips snapshot check - [INCENTIVE] BaseAuth::setStaticSignature — `_timestamp` is uint96 but cast to uint256 in storage encoding — if caller provides timestamp > 2^96, truncation wraps around, signature expires immediately - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recover — threshold read from signature, not storage — attacker can craft signature with threshold=1 even if imageHash encodes higher threshold, bypassing multisig - [INCENTIVE] BaseSig::recoverBranch — multiple FLAG_ADDRESS nodes can reference same address with different weights — imageHash includes each node separately, attacker can reuse address to reduce unique signers needed --- **Summary:** Found ~45 plausible issues across all lenses. Key concerns: unchecked arithmetic in weight accumulation, OOB reads in LibBytes, reentrancy via ERC1271/Sapient callbacks, cross-chain replay via noChainId, static signature expiry manipulation, and threshold bypasses in chained/nested signatures.

ECONOMIC / INCENTIVE LOGIC — - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::DEPOSIT_LIMIT — public mutable variable — owner could change it to bypass limit (wait, it's not mutable, but named like a constant) - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::depositTokensToL2 — no fee mechanism — users can deposit/withdraw freely, no incentive for bridge operation - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::withdrawTokensToL1 — signer can approve any withdrawal — malicious signer can drain bridge - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::sendToL1 — no value sent with transaction — if message includes ETH transfer, bridge contract must hold ETH (not funded) - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::DEPOSIT_LIMIT — limit is on vault balance not per-user — whale can block all other deposits - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::withdrawTokensToL1 — no slippage/minAmount parameter — front-running or MEV could manipulate withdrawal value - **[ECONOMIC]** L1Vault — holds all deposited tokens — single point of failure, if compromised all user funds lost - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::depositTokensToL2 — no withdrawal timelock — bridge operator could rug by immediately processing fake withdrawals - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::sendToL1 — arbitrary call with value — bridge needs ETH balance but no deposit function, where does ETH come from? - **[ECONOMIC]** TokenFactory::deployToken — deployed tokens have no link to factory — factory owner can't pause or upgrade deployed tokens - **[ECONOMIC]** L1BossBridge::withdrawTokensToL1 — signature doesn't encode amount commitment — signer could be tricked into signing different amount than user requested ---

Critical Observations — **SIGNATURE REPLAY (Critical):** `sendToL1` has no nonce, no deadline, no chain-id in the signed message — same signature can be replayed infinite times and across chains. **ARBITRARY CALL (Critical):** `sendToL1` allows signers to call ANY function on ANY contract with the bridge's authority — malicious signer can drain vault, steal tokens, or brick contracts. **DEPOSIT LIMIT BYPASS:** DEPOSIT_LIMIT checks vault balance, but withdrawals decrease balance — attacker deposits 100k, withdrawal processed, deposits another 100k, repeat. **CROSS-CHAIN DEPLOYMENT RISK:** ZKSync uses different CREATE/CREATE2 semantics — vault address will differ between Ethereum and ZKSync, breaking bridge assumptions. **NO ETH FUNDING:** `sendToL1` can send ETH (`value` parameter) but bridge has no payable functions — where does ETH come from? Function will always revert if value > 0.

TOKEN ACCOUNTING — - [TOKEN ACCOUNTING] ExplicitSessionManager::VALUE_TRACKING_ADDRESS — uses 0xEeee... for native value but no validation if user sends actual ERC20 at that address — accounting collision - [TOKEN ACCOUNTING] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateExplicitCall — call.value checked against sessionPermissions.valueLimit but no check if wallet actually has balance — could allow phantom value usage

5. ARRAY / INDEX / INPUT VALIDATION — - [ARRAY] Recovery::_recoverBranch — `while (rindex < _signature.length)` but rindex increments based on data reads — malformed data could skip boundary check - [ARRAY] SessionSig::recoverSignature — `attestationIndex >= attestationList.length` checked but attestationList constructed from untrusted `attestationCount` — could be zero-length and cause revert DOS - [ARRAY] SessionSig::recoverSignature — `callIdx` used to index `payload.calls` and `sig.callSignatures` but no explicit bounds check if they mismatch in length - [ARRAY] SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature — `payload.calls.length == 0` reverts but sessionUsageLimits array sized to calls.length — edge case if single call - [ARRAY] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateExplicitCall — `permissionIdx >= sessionPermissions.permissions.length` reverts but permissionIdx from untrusted signature — attacker controls revert path - [ARRAY] PermissionValidator::validatePermission — usageLimits array resized with `mstore(newUsageLimits, actualLimitsCount)` — if actualLimitsCount calculation wrong, could point to uninitialized memory - [ARRAY] ImplicitSessionManager::_isAddressBlacklisted — binary search assumes sorted blacklist but only checked in SessionSig::recoverConfiguration — if signature crafted to skip that check, binary search fails - [ARRAY] SessionSig::recoverConfiguration — `while (pointer < encoded.length)` but nested branches recursively call recoverConfiguration — malicious nesting could cause stack overflow or unbounded gas - [ARRAY] Recovery::queuedPayloadHashes — unbounded push to array — no mechanism to clear old entries, eventual DOS on iteration

6. EXTERNAL CALLS & REENTRANCY — - [REENTRANCY] ImplicitSessionManager::_validateImplicitCall — external call to `ISignalsImplicitMode(call.to).acceptImplicitRequest` during validation — untrusted target could reenter or manipulate state - [REENTRANCY] Recovery::isValidSignature — calls `IERC1271(_signer).isValidSignature` if signer has code — malicious contract could reenter queuePayload or manipulate timestampForQueuedPayload - [EXTERNAL CALLS] Recovery::queuePayload — calls isValidSignature which can call external IERC1271 before storage write — violates CEI pattern, reentrancy possible - [EXTERNAL CALLS] ImplicitSessionManager::_validateImplicitCall — acceptImplicitRequest call is view but target contract could have state-dependent view that changes between validation and execution

7. CROSS-CHAIN / BRIDGE — - [CROSS-CHAIN] Recovery::domainSeparator — uses `_noChainId` flag to optionally zero chainId — replay attack if user signs with noChainId=true and signature replayed on different chain - [CROSS-CHAIN] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateExplicitCall — sessionPermissions.chainId == 0 means any chain — could allow cross-chain replay if wallet deployed at same address - [CROSS-CHAIN] SessionManager::MAX_SPACE — limits nonce space to uint80 but payload.space is uint256 — inconsistent type could cause issues if migrating cross-chain

8. ECONOMIC / INCENTIVE LOGIC — - [ECONOMIC] Recovery::timestampForQueuedPayload — queued timestamp never expires or resets — old queued payloads remain valid forever if requiredDeltaTime passes - [ECONOMIC] Recovery::_recoverBranch — requiredDeltaTime checked with `block.timestamp - queuedAt >= requiredDeltaTime` — miner can manipulate timestamp by ~15 seconds to prematurely allow recovery - [ECONOMIC] ExplicitSessionManager::incrementUsageLimit — allows incrementing usage but no decrement or reset — if user wants to revoke session, usage persists and could hit limits unexpectedly - [ECONOMIC] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateLimitUsageIncrement — requires first call to be incrementUsageLimit if limits exist — user pays gas for increment even if subsequent calls fail, griefing vector - [ECONOMIC] SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature — validates all calls before any execution — if one call invalid, entire payload fails but user already paid for validation gas - [ECONOMIC] PermissionValidator::validatePermission — cumulative usage tracked per (signer, permission, ruleIndex) hash — same signer with slightly different permission gets separate limit, potential bypass - [ECONOMIC] Recovery::queuePayload — no cost to queue except gas — attacker can queue many payloads for victim's recovery signers to cause high gas cost when victim tries to iterate queuedPayloadHashes - [ECONOMIC] ExplicitSessionManager::VALUE_TRACKING_ADDRESS — native value limit enforced per session but if multiple sessions used in one payload, each has separate limit — could exceed intended total wallet value limit - [ECONOMIC] SessionSig::recoverConfiguration — permissions array pre-allocated to `encoded.length / MIN_ENCODED_PERMISSION_SIZE` — attacker can craft large encoded data with few permissions, wasting memory allocation gas - [ECONOMIC] ImplicitSessionManager::_validateImplicitCall — blacklist checked with binary search O(log n) — attacker can craft maximum-size blacklist to increase validation gas for every call - [ECONOMIC] Passkeys::recoverSapientSignatureCompact — WebAuthn.verify is expensive cryptographic operation — no rate limit, attacker can spam invalid signatures to waste gas - [ECONOMIC] Recovery::_recoverBranch — recursive branch traversal with no depth limit — attacker can craft deeply nested signature to cause high gas or stack overflow - [ECONOMIC] SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature — loops over all calls, all sessionUsageLimits, all limits — O(n²) or O(n³) complexity, attacker can craft payload with max calls and max limits per session - [ECONOMIC] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateExplicitCall — finds sessionPermissions by linear search through allSessionPermissions — O(n) per call, attacker can add many signers to increase gas - [ECONOMIC] PermissionValidator::validatePermission — loops over usageLimits array for each rule to find matching usageHash — O(rules * limits), gas grief with many rules and limits

Additional High-Severity Concerns — - [CRITICAL] SessionSig::recoverSignature — entire signature structure is user-controlled and parsed without strict validation — malformed encoding could cause reads beyond calldata bounds leading to undefined behavior - [CRITICAL] Recovery::_recoverBranch — verified flag is OR'd across branches but root is built incrementally — attacker could craft signature where one branch verifies (due to old queue) but root doesn't match expected config, accepting invalid signature - [CRITICAL] ExplicitSessionManager::_validateLimitUsageIncrement — constructs expected incrementUsageLimit call data and compares hash — attacker could frontrun with different call data that changes limits before comparison, bypassing validation - [CRITICAL] SessionManager::recoverSapientSignature — returns sig.imageHash but imageHash is built from user-controlled signature — if signature parsing has bug, wrong imageHash returned and arbitrary config accepted - [CRITICAL] ImplicitSessionManager::_isAddressBlacklisted — binary search assumes sorted array but sorting only validated once during recoverConfiguration — if memory corruption or assembly error, search returns wrong result - [CRITICAL] Passkeys::_decodeSignature — flags byte determines encoding path but 0x20 bit determines abi.decode fallback — attacker can flip bit to bypass compact encoding checks and inject arbitrary x, y coords

H-01 Chained signature path bypasses checkpointer when chained-mode flag set — Control-flow gate where a flag bypasses signature-validation chain. Chained-sig branch never invokes checkpointer guard. Citation: human_only (novel structural shape; candidate for between-contest TLA+ corpus growth).

H-02 Partial signature replay across session sub-calls — SessionSig validates each per-call signature independently without binding to batch identity / nonce / index. Citation: TLC counterexample on new spec PartialSignatureReplay.tla — Invariant BatchIntegrity violated at state 2 with SubmitBuggy(c=1, b=2).

M-01 Session signatures replay across wallets due to missing wallet binding — SessionManager-derived signing payload omits address(this) from EIP-712 domain. Citation: human_only (close to ERC4337StaticSigDoS shape but distinct; candidate for between-contest extension).

M-02 Static signatures bound to caller revert under ERC-4337 EntryPoint — msg.sender check fails on 4337 path. Citation: TLC counterexample on existing docs/tla/ERC4337StaticSigDoS.tla — Invariant Authorized4337CallsExecute violated.

M-03 BaseAuth.recoverSapientSignature returns hard-coded constant — Function body returns sentinel instead of recovered signer image hash. Citation: human_only (TLA+ structurally cannot model "function returns wrong constant").

M-04 Factory.deploy reverts when contract already exists instead of returning address — CREATE2 path lacks extcodesize pre-check. Citation: TLC counterexample on docs/tla/Create2NonIdempotent.tla — Invariant DeployedNeverReverts violated.
L-01 Incorrect intermediate validation of cumulative parameter rules — Per-call cumulative check uses wrong accumulator state. Citation: human_only.
L-02 Session value-spent counter increments on fallback and reverted calls — Counter-on-failed-path. Citation: human_only.

L-03 Nonce consumption reverts on execution failure enabling signature replay — Nonce intended as one-shot guard but update reverts with inner call. Citation: human_only (close to SignatureReplay shape; candidate for variant).
L-04 Unnecessary bitmasking in LibBytes::readUintX — Defensive bitmasks redundant. Citation: slither incorrect-shift detector at utils/LibBytes.sol:76.
